title: Prompt Engineering
description: Prompt patterns, structured outputs, function calling, context design, and prompt security.

questions.title: When would you use zero-shot versus few-shot prompting?
questions.scenario: A SaaS workflow automation team runs LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses. A recent review found that small prompt changes alter behavior and break downstream automation; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for zero-shot versus few-shot prompting measured by schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate.
questions.expectedAnswer: zero-shot versus few-shot prompting requires comparing at least two viable choices instead of assuming one technique is universally better. In LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses, the practical approach is clear instruction hierarchy, examples, schema constraints, validation, and regression tests. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from prompt versions, schemas, tool traces, validation failures, and regression datasets, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using JSON schema, function calling, Promptfoo, LangSmith, and prompt/version registries, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is prompt changes silently breaking output format or tool behavior; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to prompt changes silently breaking output format or tool behavior. zero-shot versus few-shot prompting addresses that risk through clear instruction hierarchy, examples, schema constraints, validation, and regression tests, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from prompt versions, schemas, tool traces, validation failures, and regression datasets. Implement the change with JSON schema, function calling, Promptfoo, LangSmith, and prompt/version registries, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds clear instruction hierarchy, examples, schema constraints, validation, and regression tests; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: How should you use chain-of-thought style reasoning safely?

commonMistakes: Explaining zero-shot versus few-shot prompting as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate.
Ignoring the main risk: prompt changes silently breaking output format or tool behavior.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that zero-shot versus few-shot prompting improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying zero-shot versus few-shot prompting, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A SaaS workflow automation team applied zero-shot versus few-shot prompting in LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses. The baseline suffered from prompt changes silently breaking output format or tool behavior, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented clear instruction hierarchy, examples, schema constraints, validation, and regression tests using JSON schema, function calling, Promptfoo, LangSmith, and prompt/version registries, then evaluated the change against schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for zero-shot versus few-shot prompting, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: How would you explain prompt leakage in a Prompt Engineering interview?
questions.scenario: A SaaS workflow automation team runs LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses. A recent review found that small prompt changes alter behavior and break downstream automation; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for prompt leakage measured by schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate.
questions.expectedAnswer: prompt leakage belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. In LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses, the practical approach is time-aware splits, feature availability audits, target-derived feature checks, and production simulation before training. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from prompt versions, schemas, tool traces, validation failures, and regression datasets, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Great Expectations, MLflow, feature lineage, time-based validation, and leakage unit tests, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is offline metrics inflated by information that would not exist at prediction time; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to offline metrics inflated by information that would not exist at prediction time. prompt leakage addresses that risk through time-aware splits, feature availability audits, target-derived feature checks, and production simulation before training, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from prompt versions, schemas, tool traces, validation failures, and regression datasets. Implement the change with Great Expectations, MLflow, feature lineage, time-based validation, and leakage unit tests, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in LLM workflow that classifies requests, extracts JSON, calls tools, and drafts responses, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds time-aware splits, feature availability audits, target-derived feature checks, and production simulation before training; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor schema-valid output rate, tool-call accuracy, regression pass rate, and manual correction rate so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.
